items.q: What do McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y describe?
items.correct: Two contrasting management assumptions: Theory X assumes workers dislike work and need control; Theory Y assumes workers can be self-motivated and seek responsibility.
items.misconception: Theory X is about working in a factory and Theory Y is about office work.
items.random_wrong: Theory X and Theory Y are stages of business growth.
items.why: The theories are about MANAGER assumptions, not industry or growth stages. McGregor's point is that managers' beliefs about workers shape how they manage and the outcomes they get.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.correct: Dislike work and need close control
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dislike work and need close control. “Love challenging work” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Piece rates are most associated with:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Taylor. “Herzberg” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many needs does McClelland identify?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
